finding: Trait and state affect demonstrate robust relationships with job-related attitudes; positive affect correlates positively with favorable attitudes while negative affect correlates negatively, with moderately large effect sizes ranging from .17 to .54 depending on attitude type.

Learners can infer intended word meanings from few examples by combining observed exemplars with category-level prior knowledge, solving the problem of learning without systematic negative evidence by using taxonomic and mutual exclusivity constraints.

Emotional responses to events are intensified when causes are abnormal or when easily undone counterfactual alternatives are mentally available, suggesting that surprise and regret depend on constructing contextual reference sets rather than comparing to precomputed expectations.

When outcome is known, people exaggerate its inevitability and predict greater likelihood of replication than those predicting without outcome knowledge, an effect reduced by forcing consideration of alternative outcomes.

People are systematically overconfident in extreme certainty judgments, being wrong 20-30% of the time when claiming complete certainty, despite detailed instructions and monetary incentives to calibrate confidence to accuracy.

People systematically predict others to be more risk seeking than themselves, but only when predicting for abstract individuals; the discrepancy vanishes when making predictions about concrete, vivid targets.
